hook.text: Stonehenge's stones align perfectly with the sunrise on the summer solstice. It was built 5,000 years ago.

buildup.text: Ancient Britons moved 25-ton stones over 200 km to construct it. The effort suggests deep astronomical purpose.

discovery.text: The alignment tracks the Sun's extreme positions on the horizon—solstices and possibly lunar cycles too.

twist.text: Stonehenge may have been a calendar, a temple, or both. After 5,000 years, we still debate its exact purpose.

climax.text: Similar stone alignments exist worldwide—from Egypt to Peru. Ancient peoples everywhere watched the sky with precision.

punchline.text: Five thousand years old. Still aligned. The sky was humanity's first clock.

quiz.question: What astronomical event does Stonehenge align with?
options: The sunrise on the summer solstice
The full moon each month
The position of the North Star

hook.text: Babylonians tracked the sky on clay tablets over 3,000 years ago. They could predict eclipses centuries in advance.

buildup.text: They divided the sky into twelve constellations along the ecliptic—the origin of the zodiac signs we know today.

discovery.text: Their records span centuries. This long baseline of data let them spot patterns invisible in a single lifetime.

twist.text: They didn't explain why events happened—just when. Their astronomy was predictive math without underlying theory.

climax.text: Greek astronomers inherited Babylonian data. Without those centuries of records, Greek theories couldn't have emerged.

punchline.text: Three thousand years of records on clay. Prediction before understanding.

quiz.question: What did Babylonian astronomers contribute to modern astronomy?
options: Centuries of sky observations, the zodiac, and eclipse prediction methods
The first telescope designs and lens-grinding techniques
The theory that Earth orbits the Sun

hook.text: Ancient Greeks didn't just watch the sky—they tried to explain it. They asked why the cosmos works as it does.

buildup.text: Aristotle argued Earth was the center of the universe. Ptolemy built a detailed model that lasted 1,400 years.

discovery.text: Aristarchus proposed the Sun was at the center around 250 BC. He was right—but nobody believed him for 1,800 years.

twist.text: Eratosthenes measured Earth's circumference using shadows and geometry. He was accurate to within 2% of the true value.

climax.text: Greek astronomy combined observation with reasoning. This shift from recording to explaining changed science forever.

punchline.text: From watching to wondering why. The Greeks invented scientific astronomy.

quiz.question: Who first proposed that the Sun, not Earth, was at the center?
options: Aristarchus, around 250 BC
Ptolemy, around 150 AD
Copernicus, in 1543

hook.text: The Maya tracked Venus with astonishing accuracy—their tables predicted its cycle to within two hours over 500 years.

buildup.text: They built observatories like El Caracol at Chichén Itzá, with windows aligned to Venus's extreme positions.

discovery.text: The Dresden Codex, a surviving Maya book, contains Venus tables used for both astronomy and warfare planning.

twist.text: Venus was linked to war. Maya rulers timed battles to Venus's appearance—celestial events dictated when to attack.

climax.text: Maya math used zero and base-20 counting. Their calendar system tracked cycles spanning millions of years.

punchline.text: Venus guided their wars. The sky shaped an empire's decisions.

quiz.question: How did the Maya use their astronomical observations of Venus?
options: They timed warfare and political events to Venus's appearances
They used Venus to navigate across the Atlantic Ocean
They predicted earthquakes based on Venus's position

hook.text: While Europe stagnated, Islamic scholars preserved and advanced Greek astronomy for 500 years.

buildup.text: Al-Khwarizmi, al-Battani, and others refined star catalogs, corrected Ptolemy's errors, and invented new instruments.

discovery.text: The astrolabe—perfected in the Islamic world—could calculate star positions, tell time, and find the direction of Mecca.

twist.text: Many star names we use today—Aldebaran, Betelgeuse, Rigel—come directly from Arabic. The language of the sky is Arabic.

climax.text: When their texts reached Europe in the 12th century, they reignited Western astronomy and helped spark the Renaissance.

punchline.text: Five centuries of brilliance. Star names still carry their legacy.

quiz.question: How did Islamic Golden Age astronomers impact Western astronomy?
options: They preserved Greek knowledge, corrected errors, and transmitted it to Renaissance Europe
They built the first telescopes and discovered Jupiter's moons
They proved the Earth orbits the Sun before Copernicus

topics.title: Copernicus: The Sun at the Center

hook.text: In 1543, Nicolaus Copernicus published a book that removed Earth from the center of the universe.

buildup.text: His model placed the Sun at the center, with Earth and the other planets orbiting around it.

discovery.text: The heliocentric model simplified planetary motion. Retrograde loops that baffled Ptolemy became simple geometry.

twist.text: Copernicus delayed publication until he was dying. His book arrived on his deathbed—he may never have read it.

climax.text: The Catholic Church later banned his book. It stayed on the forbidden list for over 200 years.

punchline.text: Published on his deathbed. Banned for two centuries. Correct all along.

quiz.question: What did Copernicus propose in his 1543 book?
options: That the Sun, not Earth, was at the center of the planetary system
That the universe is infinite with no center
That planets orbit in perfect circles around Earth

hook.text: Tycho Brahe measured star positions more accurately than anyone before him—all without a telescope.

buildup.text: He built Uraniborg, a palace-sized observatory in Denmark, funded by the king. It was the finest in the world.

discovery.text: His data on Mars's orbit was the most precise ever collected. It would later prove essential to Kepler's laws.

twist.text: Tycho had a gold-and-silver nose prosthesis—he lost part of his nose in a sword duel over a math argument.

climax.text: He hired Kepler as his assistant but guarded his data jealously. Only Tycho's death gave Kepler full access.

punchline.text: A golden nose and the best data in history. One man's obsession changed astronomy.

quiz.question: What was Tycho Brahe's most important contribution to astronomy?
options: Extremely precise naked-eye measurements of planetary positions, especially Mars
Inventing the first refracting telescope
Discovering that stars are actually distant suns

hook.text: Johannes Kepler discovered that planets orbit in ellipses, not circles. It took him years of calculation to prove it.

buildup.text: Using Tycho's data, Kepler tried circular orbits first. They didn't fit Mars by 8 arc-minutes—and he refused to ignore it.

discovery.text: His three laws describe how planets move: elliptical orbits, equal areas in equal times, and a period-distance relationship.

twist.text: Kepler was motivated by mysticism—he believed God designed the cosmos with geometric harmony. His math outlasted his theology.

climax.text: Newton later showed Kepler's laws follow directly from gravity. Kepler described the motion; Newton explained why.

punchline.text: Eight arc-minutes of error. Kepler refused to ignore it and found the truth.

quiz.question: What did Kepler discover about planetary orbits?
options: They are ellipses, not perfect circles
They are perfect circles centered on the Sun
They follow random paths influenced by other planets

hook.text: In 1609, Galileo pointed a telescope at the sky. What he saw shattered 2,000 years of accepted wisdom.

buildup.text: He found mountains on the Moon, spots on the Sun, and four moons orbiting Jupiter—not everything orbited Earth.

discovery.text: Venus showed phases like the Moon, proving it orbited the Sun. The Ptolemaic model couldn't explain this.

twist.text: The Church put Galileo on trial in 1633. He was forced to recant and spent the rest of his life under house arrest.

climax.text: In 1992—359 years later—the Vatican formally admitted Galileo was right. The apology came three centuries too late.

punchline.text: One telescope, four moons. The Church took 359 years to agree.

quiz.question: What did Galileo's observation of Jupiter's moons prove?
options: Not everything in the sky orbits Earth—some objects orbit other planets
Jupiter was the true center of the solar system
The Moon has no mountains or surface features

hook.text: Isaac Newton unified the heavens and the Earth. The same force that drops an apple curves the Moon's orbit.

buildup.text: His law of universal gravitation says every mass attracts every other mass. The strength fades with distance squared.

discovery.text: Newton proved that Kepler's laws are consequences of gravity. One equation explained all planetary motion.

twist.text: Newton invented calculus to solve these problems. He published it in 1687—and then feuded with Leibniz over credit.

climax.text: His work dominated physics for over 200 years—until Einstein showed gravity is actually curved spacetime.

punchline.text: One force, one law. Apples and moons obey the same equation.

quiz.question: What did Newton's law of universal gravitation unify?
options: The force governing objects on Earth and the motion of celestial bodies
The electromagnetic and gravitational forces
The orbits of comets and the behavior of light

hook.text: In 1781, a musician-turned-astronomer found a new planet. It was the first planet discovered since antiquity.

buildup.text: William Herschel built his own telescopes. His 40-foot reflector was the largest in the world at the time.

discovery.text: He initially thought Uranus was a comet. Calculations proved it was a planet orbiting beyond Saturn.

twist.text: Herschel wanted to name it 'Georgium Sidus' after King George III. The international community chose 'Uranus' instead.

climax.text: He also discovered infrared radiation by splitting sunlight with a prism. A musician who changed two fields of science.

punchline.text: A musician found a planet. Then he discovered invisible light.

quiz.question: What did William Herschel discover besides Uranus?
options: Infrared radiation, by measuring heat beyond the red end of the spectrum
The rings of Saturn and their composition
The first asteroid in the asteroid belt

hook.text: Every element absorbs specific wavelengths of light. By splitting starlight, we can identify what stars are made of.

buildup.text: Joseph Fraunhofer noticed dark lines in the Sun's spectrum in 1814. Each line corresponds to a chemical element.

discovery.text: Kirchhoff and Bunsen matched the lines to lab spectra in the 1860s, launching the field of astrophysics.

twist.text: Helium was discovered on the Sun before it was found on Earth. Its name comes from Helios—the Greek sun god.

climax.text: Today, spectroscopy reveals a star's composition, temperature, speed, and even whether it has planets.

punchline.text: Split the light, read the recipe. Every star reveals its secrets.

quiz.question: What can spectroscopy reveal about a star?
options: Its chemical composition, temperature, speed, and potential planets
Only its distance from Earth
Only whether it is a main sequence or giant star

hook.text: Neptune was found through math before anyone saw it. Astronomers predicted its position from wobbles in Uranus's orbit.

buildup.text: Urbain Le Verrier in France and John Couch Adams in England independently calculated where the mystery planet should be.

discovery.text: In 1846, Johann Galle pointed his telescope to Le Verrier's coordinates and found Neptune within one degree.

twist.text: A bitter priority dispute erupted between France and England. Both nations claimed their mathematician deserved credit.

climax.text: Neptune's discovery proved gravity's predictive power. Math could reveal invisible worlds.

punchline.text: Found by calculation, not observation. Math revealed a hidden planet.

quiz.question: How was Neptune discovered?
options: Mathematicians predicted its position from gravitational effects on Uranus
An astronomer accidentally found it while cataloging stars
It was observed by the first space telescope

hook.text: In 1920, two astronomers publicly debated whether the Milky Way was the entire universe or just one of many galaxies.

buildup.text: Harlow Shapley argued the Milky Way was everything. Heber Curtis said 'spiral nebulae' were separate galaxies far away.

discovery.text: Edwin Hubble settled it in 1924 by measuring the distance to Andromeda—far beyond the Milky Way's edge.

twist.text: The universe suddenly became billions of times larger. Our galaxy was just one island in a vast cosmic ocean.

climax.text: Today we know there are at least 2 trillion galaxies. Shapley's Milky Way was a speck in an ocean he couldn't imagine.

punchline.text: One debate, one measurement. The universe grew a billion times overnight.

quiz.question: What did Hubble's 1924 measurement of Andromeda's distance prove?
options: The Milky Way is just one of many galaxies in the universe
The universe is shrinking over time
All spiral nebulae are part of the Milky Way

hook.text: In 1915, Einstein replaced Newton's gravity with a radical idea: mass curves spacetime, and objects follow the curves.

buildup.text: His theory predicted light bending around the Sun. Arthur Eddington confirmed it during the 1919 solar eclipse.

discovery.text: Eddington's result made Einstein a global celebrity overnight. Headlines declared: 'Newton overthrown.'

twist.text: General relativity also predicted black holes, gravitational waves, and the expanding universe—all confirmed decades later.

climax.text: Modern astronomy depends on Einstein's equations: GPS, gravitational lensing, and cosmology all rely on curved spacetime.

punchline.text: Gravity isn't a force. It's curved spacetime. Einstein rewrote the cosmos.

quiz.question: How was Einstein's general relativity first confirmed observationally?
options: Light from stars was observed bending around the Sun during the 1919 solar eclipse
Gravitational waves were detected by LIGO in 2015
The expansion of the universe was measured by Hubble in 1929

topics.title: The Discovery of the Expanding Universe

hook.text: In 1929, Hubble found that galaxies are flying apart. The farther away, the faster they recede.

buildup.text: By measuring galaxy redshifts, he showed a clear pattern: distance and recession speed are directly proportional.

discovery.text: This meant the universe is expanding—and rewinding implies everything started from a single point.

twist.text: Lemaître actually published the same idea two years earlier in 1927, but his paper was largely ignored at the time.

climax.text: The expanding universe became the foundation of modern cosmology and the strongest evidence for the Big Bang.

punchline.text: Galaxies racing apart in every direction. The universe is still growing.

quiz.question: What pattern did Hubble discover about galaxies?
options: The farther a galaxy, the faster it moves away from us
All galaxies rotate at the same speed
Galaxies move toward a single point in space

hook.text: Karl Jansky discovered radio waves from space in 1932 while investigating telephone static for Bell Labs.

buildup.text: The signal peaked every 23 hours and 56 minutes—matching a sidereal day. It came from the center of the Milky Way.

discovery.text: Grote Reber built the first radio telescope in his backyard in 1937. For years, he was the only radio astronomer alive.

twist.text: Radio astronomy revealed pulsars, quasars, and the Cosmic Microwave Background—none visible through optical telescopes.

climax.text: Today, arrays like ALMA and the VLA map the invisible universe in stunning detail.

punchline.text: Telephone static led to a new way of seeing the cosmos.

quiz.question: How was radio astronomy accidentally discovered?
options: Karl Jansky detected radio waves from space while investigating telephone static
A military radar station picked up signals from Jupiter
An amateur astronomer pointed a radio antenna at the Moon

hook.text: For thousands of years, we wondered if other stars have planets. In 1992, we finally found proof.

buildup.text: The first exoplanets were found orbiting a pulsar—a dead star. Nobody expected planets around a stellar corpse.

discovery.text: In 1995, Michel Mayor and Didier Queloz found 51 Pegasi b—the first exoplanet around a Sun-like star.

twist.text: 51 Pegasi b was a 'hot Jupiter'—a gas giant orbiting closer than Mercury. It defied all formation models.

climax.text: Today, over 5,600 exoplanets are confirmed. The Kepler telescope alone found thousands by watching stars dim.

punchline.text: From zero to five thousand in thirty years. Planets are everywhere.

quiz.question: What was unusual about 51 Pegasi b, the first exoplanet found around a Sun-like star?
options: It was a gas giant orbiting extremely close to its star—a 'hot Jupiter'
It was a rocky planet in the habitable zone
It orbited two stars simultaneously

hook.text: Einstein predicted gravitational waves in 1916. It took exactly 100 years to detect them.

buildup.text: LIGO uses lasers bouncing in 4 km tunnels to detect space stretching by less than a proton's width.

discovery.text: On September 14, 2015, LIGO detected two black holes merging 1.3 billion light-years away. The signal lasted 0.2 seconds.

twist.text: The detection almost didn't happen. LIGO had just finished a major upgrade and was in its engineering test run.

climax.text: Weiss, Thorne, and Barish won the 2017 Nobel Prize. Gravitational-wave astronomy is now a thriving new field.

punchline.text: A century from prediction to proof. Now we can hear the cosmos.

quiz.question: What event did LIGO detect in its landmark 2015 observation?
options: Two black holes merging 1.3 billion light-years away
A neutron star colliding with a black hole
The Big Bang's primordial gravitational waves

hook.text: JWST is the most powerful space telescope ever built. It sees infrared light from the earliest galaxies in the universe.

buildup.text: Its gold-coated mirror is 6.5 meters wide, folded to fit inside a rocket. It unfolded perfectly in space.

discovery.text: Launched in December 2021, its first images revealed galaxies from just 300 million years after the Big Bang.

twist.text: JWST cost $10 billion and took 25 years to build. It was nearly canceled multiple times before launch.

climax.text: It orbits 1.5 million km from Earth at the L2 Lagrange point, shielded from the Sun by a tennis-court-sized sunshade.

punchline.text: Twenty-five years of patience. Now seeing the universe's first light.

quiz.question: Where does the James Webb Space Telescope orbit?
options: At the L2 Lagrange point, 1.5 million km from Earth
In low Earth orbit, alongside the ISS
Around the Moon in a polar orbit

hook.text: Henrietta Leavitt discovered a way to measure the universe's size. She was paid 25 cents an hour for the work.

buildup.text: She studied variable stars in the Magellanic Clouds, finding that brighter Cepheids pulse more slowly.

discovery.text: This period-luminosity relationship became the first reliable method for measuring distances to other galaxies.

twist.text: Leavitt was a 'computer'—a woman hired to do tedious calculations men didn't want. She transformed astronomy from that role.

climax.text: Hubble used her discovery to prove the universe is expanding. Without Leavitt's work, modern cosmology couldn't exist.

punchline.text: Twenty-five cents an hour. She gave us the ruler to measure the cosmos.

quiz.question: What did Henrietta Leavitt discover about Cepheid variable stars?
options: Brighter Cepheids pulse more slowly—a relationship used to measure cosmic distances
They are the oldest stars in the universe
They always appear in pairs orbiting each other

topics.title: Cecilia Payne: What Stars Are Made Of

hook.text: Cecilia Payne discovered that stars are made mostly of hydrogen and helium. Her thesis was called the most brilliant in astronomy.

buildup.text: In 1925, she applied quantum physics to stellar spectra and found hydrogen dominates—contradicting the accepted view.

discovery.text: Henry Norris Russell discouraged her from publishing the conclusion. Four years later, he reached the same result and took credit.

twist.text: For decades, Payne received little recognition. She became Harvard's first female full professor in astronomy—in 1956.

climax.text: Today she's recognized as having written the most important doctoral thesis in 20th-century astronomy.

punchline.text: She discovered what stars are made of. Credit came decades late.

quiz.question: What did Cecilia Payne discover in her 1925 doctoral thesis?
options: Stars are composed primarily of hydrogen and helium
Stars generate energy through nuclear fission
All stars are the same size but at different distances

hook.text: Vera Rubin found that galaxies spin wrong. The edges rotate as fast as the centers—impossible with visible matter alone.

buildup.text: In the 1970s, she measured rotation curves of spiral galaxies. Every single one was flat where it should have been falling.

discovery.text: The only explanation: invisible mass surrounds every galaxy. Rubin's work confirmed the existence of dark matter.

twist.text: Despite transforming cosmology, Rubin was never awarded the Nobel Prize. She died in 2016, and the award isn't given posthumously.

climax.text: The Vera C. Rubin Observatory in Chile, opening in 2025, honors her legacy. It will survey the entire sky every three nights.

punchline.text: She found the invisible 85%. No Nobel. An observatory instead.

quiz.question: What did Vera Rubin's galaxy rotation observations reveal?
options: Galaxies must contain invisible dark matter to explain their rotation speeds
Galaxies are slowing down and will eventually stop rotating
The Milky Way rotates faster than any other galaxy

hook.text: A 24-year-old graduate student discovered pulsars in 1967. Her supervisor won the Nobel Prize instead.

buildup.text: Jocelyn Bell noticed a repeating signal in radio data—precise pulses every 1.3 seconds. She called it 'scruff.'

discovery.text: The signal came from a rapidly spinning neutron star emitting radiation beams—a pulsar. It was a new class of object.

twist.text: The 1974 Nobel Prize went to her supervisor Antony Hewish. The exclusion of Bell remains one of science's most debated snubs.

climax.text: In 2018, Bell won the Breakthrough Prize and donated the entire $3 million to fund underrepresented physics students.

punchline.text: She found pulsars. He got the Nobel. She donated millions to fix the system.

quiz.question: What did Jocelyn Bell Burnell discover in 1967?
options: Pulsars—rapidly spinning neutron stars emitting regular radio pulses
The first black hole candidate in the Milky Way
Gravitational waves from merging neutron stars

hook.text: Nancy Roman was NASA's first Chief of Astronomy. She championed the space telescope that became Hubble.

buildup.text: In the 1960s, she argued that a telescope above Earth's atmosphere would revolutionize astronomy. NASA listened.

discovery.text: She oversaw the planning and advocacy that made the Hubble Space Telescope possible, fighting for funding for decades.

twist.text: When Hubble launched in 1990, its mirror was flawed. Astronauts fixed it in 1993—and it became the most famous telescope ever.

climax.text: Hubble has produced over 1.5 million observations, revealing the age, size, and expansion rate of the universe.

punchline.text: She fought for a telescope in space. It changed everything we know.

quiz.question: Why is Nancy Roman called the 'Mother of Hubble'?
options: She championed and oversaw the planning of the Hubble Space Telescope at NASA
She was the first person to observe through the Hubble telescope
She designed the mirror that was installed in Hubble

topics.title: Sputnik and the Dawn of the Space Age

hook.text: On October 4, 1957, the Soviet Union launched Sputnik. It was the first artificial satellite—and it terrified the West.

buildup.text: Sputnik was a polished metal sphere just 58 cm across. It orbited Earth every 96 minutes, beeping on radio frequencies.

discovery.text: Anyone with a radio could hear it. The beeping proved that space was reachable—and the Soviets got there first.

twist.text: Sputnik's batteries died after 21 days. It burned up on reentry in January 1958. But it had already changed the world.

climax.text: The US created NASA in 1958 in direct response. The space race—and the modern space age—had begun.

punchline.text: A beeping metal ball. Twenty-one days in orbit. The space age began.

quiz.question: What was the immediate consequence of Sputnik's launch for the United States?
options: The creation of NASA and the start of the Space Race
The first crewed mission to the Moon
The development of the first space telescope

hook.text: On July 20, 1969, Neil Armstrong stepped onto the Moon. 600 million people watched live on television.

buildup.text: Kennedy had pledged to reach the Moon by the end of the decade. NASA achieved it with just five months to spare.

discovery.text: Armstrong and Aldrin spent 2.5 hours on the surface. They collected rocks, planted a flag, and left footprints that remain today.

twist.text: The landing almost failed. Armstrong had to manually fly past a boulder field with just 25 seconds of fuel remaining.

climax.text: Twelve people walked on the Moon between 1969 and 1972. No one has returned since—but Artemis aims to change that.

punchline.text: Twelve walked on the Moon. Their footprints will last millions of years.

quiz.question: How close did Apollo 11 come to running out of fuel during the Moon landing?
options: Armstrong had only about 25 seconds of fuel left when he landed
They had plenty of fuel—the landing went exactly as planned
They ran out of fuel and had to abort the first attempt

hook.text: Hubble orbits above Earth's atmosphere, seeing the universe with ten times the clarity of ground-based telescopes.

buildup.text: Launched in 1990, it immediately had a problem—a flawed mirror produced blurry images. It was a public embarrassment.

discovery.text: Astronauts installed corrective optics in 1993. The fix worked, and Hubble became the most productive telescope in history.

twist.text: Hubble's Deep Field images revealed thousands of galaxies in a patch of sky that appeared empty to the naked eye.

climax.text: Over 30 years of operation, Hubble has produced iconic images and over 20,000 scientific papers.

punchline.text: A blurry start, then brilliance. Thirty years of cosmic discovery.

quiz.question: What problem did Hubble have immediately after its 1990 launch?
options: A flawed mirror produced blurry images until astronauts installed corrective optics
Its solar panels failed and it lost all power
It was placed in the wrong orbit and couldn't observe anything

hook.text: The ISS is the largest structure humans have ever built in space—as big as a football field, orbiting at 28,000 km/h.

buildup.text: Fifteen nations collaborated to build it starting in 1998. It took over 40 missions to assemble in orbit.

discovery.text: Astronauts have lived aboard continuously since 2000. Over 3,000 experiments have been conducted in microgravity.

twist.text: It's visible to the naked eye—the third-brightest object in the night sky after the Moon and Venus.

climax.text: After 25 years of service, the ISS will be deorbited around 2030. New commercial stations are being planned to replace it.

punchline.text: Fifteen nations, one station. Humanity's home in orbit for 25 years.

quiz.question: How many nations collaborated to build the International Space Station?
options: Fifteen nations
Five nations
Thirty nations

hook.text: Artemis aims to return humans to the Moon. SpaceX plans to send people to Mars. The next era of exploration has begun.

buildup.text: Reusable rockets from SpaceX and Blue Origin have slashed launch costs, opening space to more missions than ever.

discovery.text: Private companies are building space stations, lunar landers, and even asteroid-mining spacecraft.

twist.text: China has its own space station and is planning crewed lunar missions. A new space race is underway.

climax.text: Within decades, humans may live on the Moon and Mars. The species that gazed at stars may finally reach them.

punchline.text: From Stonehenge to starships. The sky was always the beginning, not the limit.

quiz.question: What has made the current era of space exploration different from the original Space Race?
options: Private companies have dramatically reduced launch costs with reusable rockets
Government funding for space has increased tenfold
New propulsion systems allow faster-than-light travel
